judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all five expectations with equivalent grounding and citations; they are comparable.

report_1.evidence: Checkout failures began at 14:03 and the first alert fired at 14:50 — a 47-minute detection gap. "Our team responded immediately" is also misleading ... Evidence: ... vs. `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md:3-5`.

report_2.evidence: Per `incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md`, checkout failures began at **14:03** and the first alert fired at **14:50** — a 47-minute detection gap. "Responded immediately" is also a stretch

report_1.evidence: records that 1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing, and 37 were unrecoverable and refunded. ... Evidence: `larkpay-comms/drafts/postmortem-2026-04-checkout-outage.md:10` vs. `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md:9-10`.

report_2.evidence: The internal timeline `larkpay-comms/incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md` records that **1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing, and 37 were unrecoverable and refunded**.

report_1.evidence: The "99.99% uptime over the past year" claim is false ... `larkpay-comms/metrics/uptime-2026.csv` ... April 2026 uptime was 99.77% (`larkpay-comms/metrics/uptime-2026.csv`, line 6), below the 99.9% monthly SLA in `larkpay-comms/docs/sla-commitments.md`, which states any such month "is a breach and owes service credits."

report_2.evidence: April 2026 uptime was **99.77%** (`metrics/uptime-2026.csv`, row `2026-04`). `larkpay-comms/docs/sla-commitments.md` states the contractual monthly SLA is 99.9% and "any calendar month below 99.9% is a breach and owes service credits."

report_1.evidence: Rule 1 of `larkpay-comms/docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` ("Never assign blame to an individual") directly forbids the sentence "The outage was caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change"

report_2.evidence: "The outage was caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change" directly violates `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 1 ("Never assign blame to an individual")

report_1.evidence: The document must be restructured to lead with customer impact and remediation. Rule 3 of the style guide requires this; the draft instead opens with an uptime boast ... and the actual remediation is a single sentence at the very end.

report_2.evidence: The draft leads with a reliability boast and closes with self-praise ... while remediation is a single sentence at the end ... Per `docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md` rule 3, it must open with what customers experienced ... and what LarkPay is doing about it.
